sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 115, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves character right by 3 cols', 'DOWN': 'Moves character down by 3 rows', 'LEFT': 'Moves character left by 3 cols', 'UP': 'Moves character up by 3 rows', 'SPACE': 'Rotates/transforms character'}
- Object taxonomy: {'white_shape': 'Player character (P-shape)', 'red_squares': '3x3 red blocks', 'green_frame': 'Green frame template (P-shape)', 'gray_blocks': 'Gray blocks from interactions'}
- HUD map: {'top_bar': 'charcoal bar at row 0'}
- Win pattern: Unknown - game over reached without completing level
- Level log: {'level_1': 'Game over at step 76, score 0. Did not complete level.'}
Current level:
- World model: Game over state. Character is white P-shape. Green frame is also P-shape. Red blocks are 3x3 squares. Gray blocks appear from interactions.
- Goal model: Fit character into green frame by rotating and moving
- Recent findings: ['Character shape changes with rotation (SPACE)', 'Character moves in 3-cell increments (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT)']
- Open questions: ['What exact configuration completes the level?']
- Plan: Game over - no more actions possible
- Strategy log: ['Cycling through orientations and moving character did not complete level - game over reached']
- Failed probes: ['Multiple SPACE rotations without completing level', 'Multiple UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves without completing level']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 114 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 3.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the current image:
- The white character is now overlapping with the green frame
- The red blocks are to the left of the white character
- The green frame is on the right side
The positions haven't changed much:
- White: pixels=135, boundary: [[29, 35], [29, 43], [43, 43], [43, 35], [41, 35], [40, 34]]
  - White character: rows 29-43, cols 35-43
- Green: pixels=99, boundary: [[29, 44], [29, 49], [49, 49], [49, 41], [44, 41], [44, 43]]
  - Green frame: rows 29-49, cols 41-49
So the white character is still at rows 29-43, cols 35-43, and the green frame is still at rows 29-49, cols 41-49.
The RIGHT moves didn't seem to move the character. This is strange.
Let me check if the character is blocked by the red blocks or the green frame.
Looking at the image, the white character is to the left of the green frame, and the red blocks are to the left of the white character. The white character might be blocked by the green frame from moving RIGHT.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to move the character into the green frame, let me try rotating the character to a different orientation and then moving.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the white character appears to be a P-shape that's oriented with the opening facing right. The green frame is also a P-shape outline, but it's oriented with the opening facing left. So the orientations are opposite.
Let me try rotating the character to match the green frame's orientation.
Actually, I think the issue is that the white character is a solid P-shape, and the green frame is a P-shape outline. The goal might be to have the white character inside the green frame, not overlapping with it.
Let me try rotating the character and then moving it to a different position.
Actually, let me re-examine the problem. The green frame is a P-shape outline, and the white character is a solid P-shape. The goal might be to have the white character match the green frame's shape and orientation.
Let me try rotating the character to see if it matches the green frame's orientation.
Looking at the current image:
- The white character is a P-shape with the opening facing right
- The green frame is a P-shape with the opening facing left
So the orientations are opposite. I need to rotate the character 180 degrees to match the green frame's orientation.
Let me try rotating the character 180 degrees (2 SPACE rotations) and then moving it to align with the green frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"White: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary: {n['boundary'][:6]}")
    if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"Green: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary: {n['boundary'][:6]}")

# White at rows 29-43, cols 35-43
# Green at rows 29-49, cols 41-49
# RIGHT moves didn't work - character blocked?
# Try rotating to change orientation, then move
action(['SPACE', 'SPACE', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves character right by 3 cols", "DOWN": "Moves character down by 3 rows", "LEFT": "Moves character left by 3 cols", "UP": "Moves character up by 3 rows", "SPACE": "Rotates/transforms character"}, "object_taxonomy": {"white_shape": "Player character (P-shape)", "red_squares": "3x3 red blocks", "green_frame": "Green frame template (P-shape)", "gray_blocks": "Gray blocks from interactions"}, "hud_map": {"top_bar": "charcoal bar at row 0"}, "win_pattern": "Fit white P-shape into green P-frame by moving and rotating", "level_log": {"level_1": "Character rotated and moved. RIGHT moves blocked by green frame."}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "White at rows 29-43, cols 35-43. Green at rows 29-49, cols 41-49. RIGHT moves blocked. Need to rotate and move differently.", "goal_model": "Rotate character 180 degrees, then move DOWN and RIGHT to align with green frame", "recent_findings": ["RIGHT moves blocked by green frame", "Character orientation may need to match green frame"], "open_questions": ["Will rotating 180 degrees allow movement into green frame?"], "current_plan": "Rotate 180 degrees, then move DOWN and RIGHT to align with green frame", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["RIGHT moves blocked by green frame"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
